title: OpenAI opens GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna to the public
subtitle: Three-tier launch ends a 13-day government-coordinated preview and puts every major frontier lab back on the public market at once.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI made GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna generally available today across ChatGPT, the OpenAI API, and Codex. The move ends the 13-day government-coordinated preview that began on June 26 with roughly 20 vetted partner organizations. The US Department of Commerce cleared the broad launch after additional testing and interagency meetings, according to OpenAI's rollout notes.

The three tiers separate cleanly by price and posture. Sol lists at $5 input and $30 output per million tokens and posts the family's strongest agentic-coding numbers, including a new state-of-the-art on Terminal-Bench 2.1. Terra runs at $2.50 and $15 with performance OpenAI positions as competitive with GPT-5.5 at roughly half the cost. Luna comes in at $1 and $6 — a budget tier below any prior OpenAI production model — and pairs with a Sol Fast mode that pushes throughput to about 750 tokens per second at $12.50 and $75. GPT-5.6 also introduces explicit prompt-cache breakpoints and a 30-minute minimum cache life, with cache writes billed at 1.25x the uncached input rate and cache reads still discounted 90 percent.

The timing matters as much as the models. For the first time since the Fable 5 export-control ban began on June 12, every major frontier AI lab has a publicly available model at the same time — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, and the leading Chinese open-weight labs. That parity resets the buying decision for enterprises that had been sitting on Sol previews, Anthropic credits, and Grok 4.5 waitlists all at once. Pricing pressure at the Luna end also puts real strain on smaller commercial models still marketed on cost.

For learners and early-career engineers: the three-tier structure is now the norm across the industry, not a quirk of any one lab. Learning to route between a cheap fast model, a mid-tier default, and a premium reasoning model — instead of picking one and defaulting to it — is quickly becoming a baseline skill for anyone building with LLMs.
